title: Red Wine on Wool
timeEstimate: Pro-lane within hours; one home round max
stainChemistry: Wool is keratin, a protein fiber held together by disulfide bonds. Hot water + agitation triggers FELTING — the disulfide bonds re-cross-link and fibers permanently mat together. This is irreversible. Red wine adds anthocyanin tannins that bond to wool keratin through hydrogen bonding. Two failure modes stack: wrong-water-temp permanently sets the tannin AND felts the fiber simultaneously. Wool's banned-tools list mirrors silk: no enzymes (digest keratin), no chlorine bleach (destroys protein), no alkaline cleaners (open scales + lock tannin), no aggressive scrubbing (felts on contact when wet). Home wool care has a one-round ceiling — repeat chemistry is the most common cause of felting.
whyThisWorks: A specialist with cold-water cycle, wool-safe detergent, and timed acid spotter can lift fresh wine without felting. Home tools work for damage prevention only — cold press-and-blot to extract bulk wine before tannin sets, then pro for everything else. Repeat home rounds on wool almost always cause felting damage worse than the original stain.
defaultAssumption: Wool + red wine = pro within hours. One home round MAX, then stop regardless of result. Repeat friction on wet wool felts the fiber permanently.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Press cold water onto the BACK of the stain with a white cloth. Vertical press only — never rub. Most fresh anthocyanin transfers in 1–2 min when caught immediately. Replace cloth as it picks up red.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Mulsolite or wool-safe NSD, cold water
spottingProtocol.instruction: Sponge cold NSD solution gently. Press only — agitation felts wool. Blot vertically. NSD suspends remaining anthocyanin without alkalinity that would weaken keratin.
Rinse until water runs clear. Residual NSD interferes with later steps. Press, don't squeeze.
Dropper-apply diluted acetic acid to residual color. Press gently. STRICT 60-second ceiling — wool weakens with prolonged acid exposure. Flush cold immediately.

spottingProtocol.agent: Dilute Hydrogen Peroxide for Oxidized Residue
spottingProtocol.instruction: If oxidized brown remains, dilute peroxide can lift it. STRICT 1–2 min on wool, monitored. Flush cold immediately.
Rinse until clear. Press between dry towels — never wring. Lay flat to air-dry, reshape while damp. NO heat, NO hanging wet.

steps: 2. Wool-safe NSD sponge press, no agitation.
4. Acetic acid spotter, 60-sec dwell, flush cold.
5. Dilute H₂O₂ (1.5% max) for oxidized residue, 1–2 min monitored.
6. Final cold rinse, press between towels, air-dry flat.
professionalProtocol.temperature: cold throughout — wool above 30°C felts when wet
warnings: NEVER hot water — felts wool AND sets tannin simultaneously
NEVER agitation / scrubbing — felts wool on contact when wet
NEVER alkaline cleaners — open scales + lock tannin
60-sec ceiling on acid; 1–2 min on dilute peroxide
Never wring wool — press between towels

homeSolutions.instruction: Wool's worst failure mode isn't the stain — it's felting from repeat home chemistry. Wool felts permanently when wet wool is agitated, when temperature crosses 30°C, when alkaline cleaners open the scales. Most home advice that "didn't work the first time, try again" causes felting damage worse than the wine. The one-round rule on wool is non-negotiable. If your one careful round doesn't clear it, pro is the answer. Don't repeat.

homeSolutions.agent: Cold Water Press-Blot from the BACK
homeSolutions.instruction: Turn inside out. Place white towel under the stain. Press a SECOND white cloth dampened in COLD/ice water onto the BACK of the stain. Vertical pressure only — no rubbing, no scrubbing, no wiping. Lift, refresh the cloth, press again. Cold water + protein-fiber rules: heat felts AND sets tannin. The two failures stack.

homeSolutions.agent: Wool-Safe Detergent — Eucalan or Soak (Optional, One Pass)
homeSolutions.instruction: If pink remains after cold water alone: mix half a teaspoon of wool-specific detergent (Eucalan or Soak) in two cups of cold water. Dip a cloth, wring nearly dry, PRESS onto the stain. 2–3 minutes. Lift, blot. ONE PASS. Do NOT repeat. Do NOT use OxiClean, Biz, regular detergent — alkalinity opens wool scales and locks tannin.

homeSolutions.agent: Press Dry + Lay Flat — DO NOT Hang Wet
homeSolutions.instruction: Press the wool between two dry white towels — stack, press, lift moisture. No wringing, no twisting. Lay flat on a fresh dry towel, reshape while damp, air-dry at room temperature out of direct sun. NEVER hang wet wool — gravity stretches the fiber. NEVER apply heat — even residual moisture + heat = felting.

homeSolutions.agent: Pro Within Hours If Anything Remains
homeSolutions.instruction: If a shadow remains after one careful cold-water round, STOP. A second home round is much more likely to felt the wool than to lift more wine. Take to a wool specialist (call ahead). Tell them: "Red wine on wool, age [hours], one round at home with cold water + [Eucalan]. No hot water, no enzymes, no OxiClean, no scrubbing." Pros use timed acetic acid + dilute peroxide with controlled dwell — won't felt, will lift.

steps: 1. Wool + red wine = ONE round at home, max.
2. Cold water press-blot from the BACK (no rubbing, no scrubbing).
3. Optional one pass with wool-safe detergent (Eucalan / Soak), 2–3 min.
4. Press dry between towels, lay flat to air-dry.
5. Anything remaining = pro within hours. NO second round.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop after one careful round. Repeat home chemistry on wool felts the fiber — damage worse than the stain.
warnings: NEVER hot water — felts wool + sets tannin
NEVER agitation / scrubbing wet wool — felts on contact
NEVER wring or hang wet wool

neverDo: Never agitate or scrub wet wool
fiberSensitivities: Wool keratin felts when wet + warm + agitated
Disulfide bonds re-cross-link irreversibly under heat
Alkaline cleaners open the scales — can't be undone

materialWarnings: ONE round home max — repeat chemistry felts wool

escalation.when: After one careful home round, regardless of result. Earlier if the spill is large, dried, or on a high-value wool garment.
escalation.whatToTell: "Red wine on wool, age [hours], one round at home with cold water + [wool-safe detergent if applicable]. No hot water, no enzymes, no OxiClean, no scrubbing. [Residual pink / brown shadow / fully clear]."
escalation.specialistType: professional dry cleaner specializing in wool (call ahead — confirm sodium hydrosulfite + acetic acid spotting capability)

contextQuestions: How long ago — fresh, hours, or older?
Wool type — merino, regular knit, woven, felt?
Has anyone used hot water / OxiClean / agitation already?
How valuable / sentimental is the garment?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Merino is finer than standard wool — felts faster. Felt fabric is already pre-felted, so additional felting risk is lower but tannin removal is harder. Hot-water / OxiClean / agitation pre-applied = wool may already be partially felted; disclose to pro. Repeat home rounds are the single most common cause of permanent damage on wool.
